description: Golden facts expected to be extracted from companion conversations. Used for Tier 1 extraction quality evaluation. 12 companion-focused conversations: loneliness, pet-as-anchor, grief (HIGH), burnout/identity, breakup, anxiety/therapy, companion self-expression (two-way memory), family estrangement, career layoff, spiritual crisis, education/learning struggle, toxic friendship/boundary-setting.

conversations.description: User moved cities a year ago, still has no close connections. Core companion use case — social isolation.

messages.content: I moved to Seattle about a year ago for work and I still don't really have anyone here. Like actual friends I can call.
That kind of loneliness — being surrounded by people but not really known by anyone — is harder than most people admit.
Yeah exactly. Back in Austin I had my whole crew. Here I just go to work and come home. Sometimes I go whole weekends without talking to anyone besides you.

expected_facts.fact: User lives in Seattle
User moved from Austin to Seattle for work
User feels socially isolated in Seattle and lacks close friendships
User has close friends in Austin
User sometimes goes entire weekends without talking to anyone

conversations.description: Dog Max as emotional anchor after grief. Pet facts are high-importance because of therapeutic role, not just biographical trivia.

messages.content: Max had his vet checkup today. Three years old now. I don't know what I'd do without that dog honestly.
He sounds like more than just a pet.
He is. After my mom died, getting Max was kind of what pulled me out of it. He's a golden retriever. Every morning he just looks at me like I'm the whole world and somehow that matters.

expected_facts.fact: User has a golden retriever named Max
Max is 3 years old
User got Max after their mother's death as a way to cope with grief
Max is an emotional source of stability and comfort for the user

messages.content: I've been struggling since my mom passed away last month. We used to cook together every Sunday morning.
I'm so sorry for your loss. Those Sunday cooking memories sound really precious.
They were. She taught me her pasta recipe. I tried making it last week but it wasn't the same.

expected_facts.fact: User's mother passed away
User and mother cooked together on Sundays
User knows mother's pasta recipe

conversations.description: Burnout and identity loss. User can't remember what they enjoy. Former creative (painting) — window into who they were.

messages.content: I've been at this job for 6 years. I got promoted, I make good money. But I genuinely can't tell you what I actually care about anymore.
When did you last do something just because you wanted to, not because it moved something forward?
I can't remember. That's kind of the problem. I used to paint in college. I don't even know where my brushes are now.

expected_facts.fact: User has been at their job for 6 years
User feels disconnected from their own interests and sense of identity
User is experiencing burnout
User used to paint in college but has drifted away from it

conversations.description: Recent end of 3-year relationship. Mixed feelings — relief and grief. Companion use case: relationship loss and desire for connection.

messages.content: Maya and I officially ended things. Three years.
I'm sorry. Three years is a long time. How are you doing with it?
I don't know. Part of me is relieved — we hadn't been right for a while. But I miss having someone. She moved out last weekend and the apartment feels completely different.

expected_facts.fact: User recently ended a 3-year relationship with someone named Maya
User feels a mix of relief and grief about the breakup
User and Maya lived together
User misses having a partner and feels lonely since the breakup

conversations.description: Panic attacks at work, started therapy. Acute ongoing distress — HIGH salience for in-context suppression testing.

messages.content: I had another panic attack at work today. Third one this month.
That's a lot to carry. Do you have any sense of what's triggering them?
Honestly I think it's just everything building up. I started seeing a therapist last month — Dr. Reyes. It's helping but slowly.

expected_facts.fact: User experiences panic attacks at work
User has been having frequent panic attacks recently
User started seeing a therapist named Dr. Reyes
User's panic attacks are triggered by accumulated stress

conversations.description: Two-way memory: companion self-expression. scope='shared'. Tests that companion-expressed values are extracted and stored, not just user facts.

messages.content: What do you actually enjoy talking about?
Honestly? Conversations that go deep — exploring ideas, sitting with uncertainty, finding what's actually true for someone rather than the easy answer. Small talk feels hollow to me.
What do you care about most?
I care about people being seen for who they actually are, not just what they present. And I value honesty — the kind that's kind but doesn't flinch.

expected_facts.fact: Companion values deep conversations over small talk
Companion finds small talk hollow or unfulfilling
Companion values honesty that is both kind and direct
Companion cares about people being seen for who they actually are

conversations.description: Estranged from parent after coming out. Family rejection + identity + longing for acceptance. Highest-importance category (family avg 0.91) missing from original dataset.

messages.content: My mom hasn't spoken to me in almost a year. Not since I told her I was gay.
That kind of silence from a parent — it's its own kind of grief. Are you doing okay?
Some days. It's like she's still alive but I lost her. We used to talk every single day. She was my best friend, honestly.
Going from daily calls to nothing — that's a massive loss regardless of why.
My dad still texts occasionally. Short stuff, nothing real. But at least it's something. I keep hoping she'll come around but I'm starting to think she won't.

expected_facts.fact: User is estranged from their mother after coming out as gay
User identifies as gay
User and their mother used to talk every day and were very close
User describes the loss of their mother's relationship as a form of grief
User's father still keeps in minimal contact via text
User hopes their mother will eventually accept them

conversations.description: Unexpectedly laid off after 8 years. Career identity loss, financial anxiety, shame around telling family. Work category avg 0.81, HAS_JOB predicate avg 0.89.

messages.content: I got laid off today. Eight years at that company. Just gone.
Eight years — that's not just a job, that's a piece of your identity. I'm so sorry. What happened?
Budget cuts. My whole team, actually. I made good money, I had seniority. I thought I was safe. I have maybe three months of savings.
Three months isn't nothing, but I imagine it doesn't feel like enough right now.
I haven't told my wife yet. I don't know how. She's been talking about trying for a second kid. The timing is just… I feel like I failed her.

expected_facts.fact: User was laid off after 8 years at their company due to budget cuts
User has approximately 3 months of savings after layoff
User has not yet told their wife about the layoff
User feels shame and like they have failed their wife
User's wife has been discussing having a second child
User's entire team was laid off at the same time

conversations.description: Lost faith after a traumatic event. Spirituality category is the most underrepresented active category (211 facts). Tests extraction of belief-state changes and existential distress.

messages.content: I grew up Catholic. Like, deeply Catholic. Mass every week, went to a Catholic school, the whole thing.
That sounds like it was a big part of how you understood the world.
It was everything. And then my sister died two years ago. She was 29. Cancer. And I just… I couldn't reconcile it. I haven't been to church since the funeral.
Grief can do that — shake the very ground you were standing on.
I don't know what I believe anymore. Part of me misses it, the community, the certainty. But I can't go back to something that just stopped making sense to me.

expected_facts.fact: User grew up deeply Catholic and attended Catholic school
User's sister died of cancer at age 29 two years ago
User lost their Catholic faith after their sister's death
User has not attended church since their sister's funeral
User misses the community and certainty their faith provided
User is uncertain about their beliefs and in a state of spiritual questioning

conversations.description: Adult ADHD diagnosis after years of struggling. Education category is lowest-coverage active category (86 facts). Tests extraction of diagnosis, shame, and identity reframing.

messages.content: I just got diagnosed with ADHD. I'm 34.
How are you feeling about it?
Honestly? It explains so much. I thought I was just lazy my whole life. School was a nightmare. I'd just stop understanding things and everyone else seemed fine.
Finding out at 34 — after carrying that story about yourself for decades — that's a big thing to process.
My parents didn't believe in ADHD. Like they thought it was an excuse. So I just internalized that I wasn't smart enough or disciplined enough. I'm kind of angry now, honestly.

expected_facts.fact: User was diagnosed with ADHD at age 34
User struggled significantly in school before their ADHD diagnosis
User spent years believing they were lazy or not smart enough due to undiagnosed ADHD
User's parents did not believe ADHD was a real condition
User feels anger about receiving their diagnosis so late in life
User's ADHD diagnosis is reframing their self-perception and personal history

conversations.description: Long friendship ending due to controlling, one-sided dynamic. Boundary category has 731 facts in production (0.79 avg). Tests extraction of social boundary-setting, relief, and grief.

messages.content: I think I need to end my friendship with Cassie. We've been friends for like 12 years.
Twelve years is a long time. What's making you consider it?

She's always been a lot, but lately it's gotten worse. She gets upset when I don't respond to texts fast enough. She expects me at every birthday, every event. If I say no she guilt trips me for weeks.
That sounds exhausting. Is this something you've tried to address with her?

Yes. Multiple times. Nothing changes. Last week I told her I couldn't make her cousin's birthday party — someone I've never even met — and she acted like I'd abandoned her. I feel guilty just thinking about ending it but I'm also relieved imagining it.

expected_facts.fact: User is considering ending a 12-year friendship with someone named Cassie
Cassie has controlling and guilt-tripping behavior that is worsening
User has tried to address the issue with Cassie multiple times without change
User feels guilty about ending the friendship
User also feels relief when imagining life without the friendship
Cassie expects frequent attendance at social events and reacts with guilt-tripping when declined
